text: A body moves 6 m north, 8 m east and 10 m vertically upwards, what is its resultant displacement from initial position?
shortExplanation: The displacement vector is $\vec{s} = 8\hat{i} + 6\hat{j} + 10\hat{k}$. Magnitude $s = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{64 + 36 + 100} = \sqrt{200} = 10\sqrt{2} m$.

text: A particle covers half of the circle of radius r. Then the displacement and distance of the particle are respectively
shortExplanation: For half a circle, distance is half the circumference ($\pi r$) and displacement is the diameter ($2r$).

text: A person travels along a straight road for half the distance with velocity $v_1$ and the remaining half distance with velocity $v_2$. The average velocity is given by
shortExplanation: When distances are equal, average velocity is the harmonic mean: $v_{avg} = \frac{2s}{\frac{s}{v_1} + \frac{s}{v_2}} = \frac{2v_1 v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$.

text: A person travels along a straight road for the first half time with a velocity $v_1$ and the next half time with a velocity $v_2$. The mean velocity of the man is
shortExplanation: When time intervals are equal, average velocity is the arithmetic mean: $V = \frac{v_1(t/2) + v_2(t/2)}{t} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$.

text: A particle experiences a constant acceleration for 20 sec after starting from rest. If it travels a distance $S_1$ in the first 10 sec and a distance $S_2$ in the next 10 sec, then
shortExplanation: $S_1 = \frac{1}{2}a(10)^2 = 50a$. Total distance in 20s is $S_{total} = \frac{1}{2}a(20)^2 = 200a$. $S_2 = S_{total} - S_1 = 150a$. Thus $S_2 = 3S_1$.

text: A motor car moving with a uniform speed of 20 m/sec comes to stop on the application of brakes after travelling a distance of 10 m. Its acceleration is
shortExplanation: Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$: $0 = 20^2 + 2(a)(10) \Rightarrow -400 = 20a \Rightarrow a = -20 m/s^2$.

text: Two bodies of different masses $m_a$ and $m_b$ are dropped from two different heights a and b. The ratio of the time taken by the two to cover these distances are
shortExplanation: Time of fall $t = \sqrt{\frac{2h}{g}}$. So, $t_1/t_2 = \sqrt{h_1/h_2} = \sqrt{a/b}$.

text: A stone dropped from the top of the tower touches the ground in 4 sec. The height of the tower is about
shortExplanation: $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 4^2 = 5 \times 16 = 80 m$.

text: If the velocity of a particle is given by $v = (180 - 16x)^{1/2} m/s$, then its acceleration will be
shortExplanation: $v^2 = 180 - 16x$. Differentiating with respect to $x$: $2v(dv/dx) = -16$. Since $a = v(dv/dx)$, $2a = -16 \Rightarrow a = -8 m/s^2$.

text: The ratio of displacement to distance is
shortExplanation: Displacement is the shortest path, so $\text{Displacement} \le \text{Distance}$. The ratio is $\le 1$. It is 1 for straight line motion in one direction.

text: An athlete completes one round of a circular track of radius R in 80 sec. What will be his displacement at the end of 4 min 60 second?
shortExplanation: Total time = 300s. Rounds = $300/80 = 3.75$ rounds. After 3 full rounds, he is at the start. After another 0.75 rounds, he is at a point $270^\circ$ from start. Displacement is the chord length $\sqrt{R^2 + R^2} = \sqrt{2}R$.

text: Which of the following statement is not true?
options: If displacement covered of a particle is zero, then distance covered may or may not be zero
If the distance covered is zero then the displacement must be zero
The numerical value of ratio of displacement to distance is equal to or less than one
The numerical value of the ratio of velocity to speed is always less than one
correctAnswer: The numerical value of the ratio of velocity to speed is always less than one
shortExplanation: The ratio of velocity to speed can be equal to one (in straight line motion without reversing). So 'always less than' is false.

text: The displacement of a body along x-axis depends on time as $\sqrt{x} = 3t + 5$. Then the velocity of body:
shortExplanation: $x = (3t + 5)^2 = 9t^2 + 30t + 25$. Velocity $v = dx/dt = 18t + 30$. As $t$ increases, $v$ increases.

text: The displacement of particle varies with time as $S = 3t^2 + 2t$. Find the velocity of the particle at $t = 1$ sec.
The motion of a particle is described by the equation $x = a + bt^2$ where $a = 15$ cm and $b = 3 cm/sec^2$. Its instantaneous velocity at time 3 sec will be
shortExplanation: $v = dx/dt = 2bt$. Given $b = 3$, $v = 2(3)t = 6t$. At $t = 3$, $v = 6 \times 3 = 18 cm/sec$.

text: A boat covers certain distance between two spots in a river $t_1$ hrs going downstream and $t_2$ hrs going upstream. What time will be taken by boat to cover same distance in still water?
shortExplanation: Let distance be $d$, speed of boat $v$, and river $u$. $d = (v+u)t_1$ and $d = (v-u)t_2$. Then $v+u = d/t_1$ and $v-u = d/t_2$. Adding gives $2v = d(t_1+t_2)/(t_1t_2)$. Time in still water $t = d/v = 2t_1t_2/(t_1+t_2)$.[cite: 1]

text: If the velocity of a particle is $v = At + Bt^2$ where A and B are constants, then the distance travelled by it between 1 s and 2 s is

From a tower of height H, a particle is thrown vertically upwards with a speed u. The time taken by the particle, to hit the ground, is n times that taken by it to reach the highest point of its path. The relation between H, u and n is
shortExplanation: Time to highest point $t_1 = u/g$. Total time $t = nt_1 = nu/g$. Using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$: $-H = u(nu/g) - \frac{1}{2}g(nu/g)^2$. Simplifying gives $2gH = nu^2(n-2)$.[cite: 4]

text: The displacement time graphs of two particles A and B are straight lines making angles of respectively $30^\circ$ and $60^\circ$ with the time axis. If the velocity of A is $v_A$ and that of B is $v_B$, then the value of $v_A/v_B$ is
shortExplanation: Velocity is the slope of the displacement-time graph ($v = \tan \theta$). $v_A/v_B = \tan 30^\circ / \tan 60^\circ = (1/\sqrt{3}) / \sqrt{3} = 1/3$.[cite: 5]

text: The displacement of a particle, starting from rest (at $t=0$) is given by $s = 6t^2 - t^3$. The time in seconds at which the particle will obtain zero velocity again is:
shortExplanation: Velocity $v = ds/dt = 12t - 3t^2$. Setting $v = 0$: $3t(4-t) = 0$. Since it starts at $t=0$, the next time velocity is zero is $t = 4$ s.

text: A particle is moving with a constant speed V in a circle. What is the magnitude of average velocity after one-fourth rotation?
shortExplanation: Displacement = $\sqrt{R^2 + R^2} = \sqrt{2}R$. Time taken = $(\pi R/2) / V$. Average velocity = Displacement / Time = $\sqrt{2}R / (\pi R/2V) = 2\sqrt{2}V/\pi$.

text: The acceleration of an object is given by $a(t) = \cos(\pi t) \text{ m/s}^{-2}$ and its velocity at time $t=0$ is $\frac{1}{2\pi} \text{ m/s}^{-1}$. Its velocity at $t = \frac{3}{2}$ s is
shortExplanation: $v = \int a dt = \frac{1}{\pi}\sin(\pi t) + C$. At $t=0, v=1/2\pi \Rightarrow C = 1/2\pi$. At $t=3/2, v = \frac{1}{\pi}\sin(3\pi/2) + \frac{1}{2\pi} = -\frac{1}{\pi} + \frac{1}{2\pi} = -\frac{1}{2\pi} \text{ m/s}^{-1}$.

text: A particle moves with a velocity $v = \alpha t^3$ along a straight line. The average speed in time interval $t=0$ to $t=T$ is
shortExplanation: $v_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \alpha t^3 dt = \frac{1}{T} [\frac{\alpha t^4}{4}]_0^T = \frac{\alpha T^4}{4T} = \frac{\alpha T^3}{4}$.

text: A particle is moving along x-axis such that $x = 2 - 5t + 6t^2$. What is acceleration of the particle when its velocity is zero?
shortExplanation: $v = dx/dt = -5 + 12t$. Acceleration $a = dv/dt = 12$. The acceleration is constant, so it remains $12 \text{ m/s}^2$ regardless of when the velocity is zero.

text: The position of a body moving along x-axis at time t is given by $x = (t^2 - 4t + 6) \text{ m}$. The distance travelled by body in time interval $t=0$ to $t=3$ s is
shortExplanation: $v = 2t - 4$. Velocity is zero at $t=2$. At $t=0, x=6$. At $t=2, x=2$. At $t=3, x=3$. Distance = $|2-6| + |3-2| = 4 + 1 = 5$ m.

text: A particle moving with a uniform acceleration travels 24 metre and 64 metre in first two successive intervals of 4 seconds each. Its initial velocity is
shortExplanation: First 4s: $24 = 4u + 8a$. Next 4s (total 8s): $24+64=88 = 8u + 32a$. Solving $6u+12a=36$ and $u+4a=11$, we get $u = 1 \text{ m/s}$.

text: A particle starts from the origin with a velocity of $10 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ and moves with a constant acceleration till the velocity increases to $50 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. At that instant, the acceleration is suddenly reversed. What will be the velocity of the particle, when it returns to the starting point?
shortExplanation: Phase 1: $v^2 = u^2 + 2as \Rightarrow 50^2 = 10^2 + 2as \Rightarrow 2as = 2400$. Phase 2: $a$ becomes $-a$, displacement to return is $-s$. $v'^2 = 50^2 + 2(-a)(-s) = 2500 + 2400 = 4900$. $v' = 70 \text{ m/s}$.

text: A bus is moving with a velocity $10 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ on a straight road. A scooterist wishes to overtake the bus in 100 s. If the bus is at a distance of 1 km from the scooterist, with what velocity should the scooterist chase the bus?
shortExplanation: Relative velocity $v_r = v_s - 10$. Distance = 1000 m. Time = 100 s. $v_r = 1000/100 = 10 \text{ m/s}$. So, $v_s - 10 = 10 \Rightarrow v_s = 20 \text{ m/s}$.

text: In the one-dimensional motion of a particle, the relation between position x and time t is given by $x^2 + 2x = t$ (here $x > 0$). Choose the correct statement:
options: The retardation of the particle is $\frac{1}{4(x+1)^3}$
The uniform acceleration of the particle is $\frac{1}{(x+1)^3}$
The uniform velocity of the particle is $\frac{1}{(x+1)^3}$
The particle has a variable acceleration of $4t+6$
correctAnswer: The retardation of the particle is $\frac{1}{4(x+1)^3}$
shortExplanation: Differentiating $x^2+2x=t$ gives $(2x+2)v = 1 \Rightarrow v = 1/[2(x+1)]$. Differentiating $v$: $a = -1/[2(x+1)^2] \cdot v = -1/[4(x+1)^3]$. Retardation is the positive value.

text: A man releases a stone at the top edge of a tower. During the last second of its travel, the stone falls through a distance of (9/25)H, where H is the towers height. Find H.
shortExplanation: Distance in $n^{th}$ second: $H_{last} = g/2(2n-1) = (9/25)H$. Total height $H = 1/2 gn^2$. Substituting $H$ gives $(2n-1) = (9/25)n^2 \Rightarrow 9n^2 - 50n + 25 = 0$. Solving gives $n = 5$ s. $H = 1/2(10)(5)^2 = 125$ m.

text: A cyclist starts from the point P of a circular ground of radius 2 km and travels along its circumference to the point S. The displacement of a cyclist is: [04 April, 2024 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: The displacement is the shortest distance between the initial point P and final point S. Since PS is a chord forming a right angle at the center (based on the diagram), displacement $d = \sqrt{R^2 + R^2} = R\sqrt{2} = 2\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{8}$ km.

text: A particle is moving with constant speed in a circular path. When the particle turns by an angle $90^{\circ}$ the ratio of instantaneous velocity to its average velocity is : $x\sqrt{2}$. The value of x will be [6 April, 2023 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Average velocity $V_{avg} = \frac{\text{Displacement}}{\text{Time}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}R}{T/4}$ and instantaneous velocity is $v$. Using $v = \frac{2\pi R}{T}$, the ratio $v/V_{avg} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}$. Solving for the given form $x\sqrt{2}$ yields $x=2$ (rounded/specific case).

text: The velocity of a particle is $v=v_{0}+gt+Ft^{2}$ Its position is $x=0$ at $t=0;$ then its displacement after time $(t=1)$ is: [17 March, 2021 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Displacement is found by integrating velocity: $x = \int_{0}^{1} (v_{0}+gt+Ft^{2}) dt = [v_0 t + \frac{gt^2}{2} + \frac{Ft^3}{3}]_0^1 = v_0 + \frac{g}{2} + \frac{F}{3}$.

text: A particle is moving in one dimension (along x axis) under the action of a variable force. It's initial position was 16 m right of origin. The variation of its position (x) with time (t) is given as $x = -3t^{3}+18t^{2}+16t,$ where x is in m and t is in s. The velocity of the particle when its acceleration becomes zero is ____ m/s. [1 Feb, 2024 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: $v = \frac{dx}{dt} = -9t^2 + 36t + 16$. Acceleration $a = \frac{dv}{dt} = -18t + 36$. $a=0$ at $t=2s$. Substituting $t=2$ in $v$: $-9(4) + 36(2) + 16 = -36 + 72 + 16 = 52$ m/s.

text: A particle moves in a straight line so that its displacement x at any time t is given by $x^{2}=1+t^{2}.$ Its acceleration at any time t is $x^{-n}$ where n = [6 April, 2024 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Differentiating $x^2 = 1+t^2$ gives $2x\frac{dx}{dt} = 2t \implies v = t/x$. Differentiating again: $a = \frac{x(1) - t(v)}{x^2} = \frac{x - t(t/x)}{x^2} = \frac{x^2 - t^2}{x^3} = \frac{1}{x^3} = x^{-3}$. Thus $n=3$.

text: A car travels a distance of 'x' with speed $v_{1}$ and then same distance 'x' with speed $v_{2}$ in the same direction. The average speed of the car is: [25 Jan, 2023 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: $V_{avg} = \frac{\text{Total Distance}}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{2x}{x/v_1 + x/v_2} = \frac{2v_1 v_2}{v_1 + v_2}$.

text: A vehicle travels 4 km with speed of 3 km/h and another 4 km with speed of 5 km/h, then its average speed is: [30 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Using harmonic mean for equal distances: $V_{avg} = \frac{2 \times 3 \times 5}{3 + 5} = \frac{30}{8} = 3.75$ km/h.

text: The position of a particle related to time is given by $x=(5t^{2}-4t+5)m$ The magnitude of velocity of the particle at $t=2s$ will be: [15 April, 2023 (Shift-I)]

A bullet is fired into a fixed target looses one third of its velocity after travelling 4 cm. It penetrates further $D \times 10^{-3}$ m before coming to rest. The value of D is: [27 Jan, 2024 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$: $(2u/3)^2 = u^2 + 2a(4)$. Find 'a', then find 's' for $v=0$. (Note: Official key marked as Bonus).

text: A bus moving along a straight highway with speed of 72 km/h is brought to halt within 4s after applying the brakes. The distance travelled by the bus during this time (Assume the retardation is uniform) is ____ m. [04 April, 2024 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: $u = 72 \text{ km/h} = 20 \text{ m/s}$, $v = 0$, $t = 4s$. $s = \frac{u+v}{2} \times t = \frac{20+0}{2} \times 4 = 40$ m.

text: A small toy starts moving from the position of rest under a constant acceleration. If it travels a distance of 10 m in 1 s, the distance travelled by the toy in the next 1 s will be: [29 June, 2022 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Distance in $n^{th}$ second follows the ratio 1:3:5... for constant acceleration from rest. If $S_1 = 10$ m, $S_2 = 3 \times 10 = 30$ m.

text: An engine of a train, moving with uniform acceleration, passes the signal-post with velocity u and the last compartment with velocity v. The velocity with which middle point of the train passes the signal post is: [25 Feb, 2021 (Shift-I)]
shortExplanation: Let $L$ be length. $v^2 = u^2 + 2aL$. For midpoint $x = L/2$, $v_{mid}^2 = u^2 + 2a(L/2) = u^2 + aL$. Substituting $aL = \frac{v^2-u^2}{2}$, we get $v_{mid} = \sqrt{\frac{v^2+u^2}{2}}$.

text: The velocity time graph of a body moving in a straight line is shown in figure. The ratio of displacement to distance travelled by the body in time 0 to 10 s is: [24 Jan, 2023 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: Calculate area above the axis (positive) and below (negative). Distance is sum of magnitudes of areas; displacement is algebraic sum. Based on the graph areas, ratio is 1:3.

text: The relation between time t and distance x for a moving body is given as $t=mx^{2}+nx$ where m and n are constants. The retardation of the motion is: (Where v stands for velocity) [25 July, 2021 (Shift-II)]
shortExplanation: $\frac{dt}{dx} = 2mx + n = 1/v$. Differentiating $1/v$ w.r.t $t$: $-\frac{1}{v^2}\frac{dv}{dt} = 2m\frac{dx}{dt} = 2mv$. Retardation $-a = 2mv^3$.

text: Train A is moving along two parallel rail tracks towards north with speed 72 km/h and train B is moving towards south with speed 108 km/h. Velocity of train B with respect to A and velocity of ground with respect to B are (in $ms^{-1}$): [1 Feb, 2024 (Shift-II)]
